title: Chunky Console Table Desk with Flip-Down Keyboard Tray
summary: A chunky 1x12 console that doubles as a desk — the front panels flip down on Euro hinges to become a keyboard tray and to open leg storage. Clever, hidden-hardware build.
description:
This is a console table with a secret: it works as a slim desk. The front-facing 1x6 panels aren't fixed — they're hung on frameless Euro hinges so they flip down. The center panel becomes a keyboard tray, and the two leg panels swing open to reveal hidden storage in the thick legs. Closed, it reads as a solid chunky console; open, it's a workstation.

The whole thing is 1x12 and 1x6, and the plan is refreshingly relaxed about fasteners — brad nails (cheapest, easiest to hide), 2" self-tapping screws, or pocket holes, in any combination. Pocket holes make the strongest joints where it counts; brads are fine where they don't. The one piece of real hardware is the six Euro hinges, and installing them early is the key insight: once the box is assembled it's very hard to get a drill inside, so hang the hinges before you close things in.

A nice touch: the 4" divider pieces double as spacers to set your shelf height during assembly. The dividers inside are positioned to hold 8-1/2" x 11" paper, but they're not structural, so you can move them. If the flip-down keyboard tray needs more support, the plan suggests a hidden chain or hinge support on the dividers, and enclosing the back with 1x6s adds overall rigidity at a little extra cost.

tools.note: For the Euro hinge cups and screws — install hinges before the box is closed in.
Optional — makes the strongest joints where you want them.

materials.name: 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Keyboard fold-down and leg storage doors.

cutList.note: Doubles as a shelf-height spacer during assembly.

steps.title: Attach the legs to the shelf
steps.body: Attach the legs to the desk shelf with your choice of fastener — brad nails and glue, 2" screws, or pocket holes, in any combination. Pocket holes make the strongest joint here and are recommended. Tip: use one of the 4" divider pieces as a temporary spacer to set your shelf height consistently.

steps.title: Install the Euro hinges now
steps.body: Install the six Euro hinges at this stage — it's much harder to get a drill inside the project as it closes in. Screw them to the project with the flat front flush to the front edges; a scrap block helps hold them flush. Exact placement isn't critical, just close to the diagram.

steps.title: Attach the inner legs and top
steps.body: Attach the inner leg pieces to the shelf — pocket holes recommended for strength. Then attach the top to the outer legs; 1-1/4" brad nails with glue are ideal here since they're the easiest to hide on a visible top surface.

steps.title: Add the dividers
steps.body: Attach the dividers inside the project with 1-1/4" brads and glue. They're spaced to fit an 8-1/2" x 11" book or paper in each opening, but they're not structural — move them wherever suits what you'll store without weakening anything.

steps.title: Hang the fold-down faces
steps.body: Position the 1x6 fold-down faces under the project — the center one is the keyboard tray, the two leg panels are storage doors — and attach them through the hinges with screws. They should swing down cleanly and sit flush when closed.

steps.title: Reinforce if needed and finish
steps.body:
If the piece needs more support, enclose the back with 1x6 boards (attached with 1-1/4" brads and glue) to add rigidity. The keyboard fold-down can be further strengthened with a hidden chain or hinge support on the insides of the dividers, which stops it from swinging too far.

Sand and finish or paint.

images.alt: Chunky wood console desk with flip-down keyboard tray and hidden leg storage
